video_title: The Most Important Crypto Tech Nobody Understands ft. Rand Hindi from Zama
gist: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama) argues that as finance moves on-chain, privacy via FHE on public chains—not private chains—is the inevitable encryption layer, the 'HTTPS for on-chain finance.' He frames real-world-asset tokenization (T-REX/Apex), B2B payments, and institutional trading as the demand drivers, positions FHE as already post-quantum, and makes a contrarian case that instant settlement should give way to latency as a security feature.

themes.theme: Public chains + FHE privacy vs. private chains

insights.claim: Zama positions itself as a confidentiality/encryption layer added on top of public chains (Ethereum, Solana) rather than a private chain, on the thesis that if finance moves on-chain it must be private—analogous to HTTPS being added to the public internet.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Frames privacy as a non-optional layer of on-chain finance; watch FHE-on-public-chain as the structural winner over private-chain approaches.
insights.quote: If we seriously think that finance is moving on chain... there is no world in which this cannot be private.

insights.claim: Hindi argues private institutional chains (e.g. Canton) are the 'intranets' of crypto—useful for internal transactions but destined to be eclipsed by public chains with an encryption layer, just as intranets were eclipsed by the encrypted public internet.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Bearish framing for permissioned/private-chain plays (Canton et al.) relative to public-chain privacy infrastructure; a directional bet for infra investors.
insights.quote: if you're comparing Kanton, an internet with Zama on Ethereum, a public network with HTTPS

insights.claim: Hindi claims Zama has never seen a single institutional customer evaluating any privacy technology other than Zama for public chains—the only competing approach institutions consider is private networks like Canton.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Implies a near-monopoly competitive moat in public-chain confidentiality if true; key claim to verify against ZK-privacy competitors.
insights.quote: we have never seen a single customer that we spoke to considering another privacy technology for public chains than Zama

insights.claim: Zama intends to be a horizontal, cross-chain privacy layer (live on Ethereum mainnet, expanding to multiple chains in 2026 including Solana and non-EVM ecosystems), aiming to make adding a new chain only a few days of work by next year.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Cross-chain composability of shielded assets is the expansion vector; multi-chain rollout cadence in 2026 is a milestone to track.

themes.theme: RWA tokenization as the demand driver

insights.claim: Zama is the confidentiality layer for the T-REX protocol, launched by Apex (a ~$3 trillion asset management group), which Hindi says is targeting tokenizing $100 billion of assets within 12 months and going to mainnet with $100B TVL on day one.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: A single named pipeline ($100B RWA via T-REX/Apex) dwarfs current on-chain RWA; concrete catalyst to monitor for Zama adoption and RWA-infra theses.
insights.quote: T-Rex is tokenizing a hundred billion dollar of assets in the next 12 months. So, T-Rex is going to production on mainet with a hundred billion dollar of TVL on day one.

insights.claim: Hindi estimates Ethereum currently holds only ~$20 billion of RWA TVL, so capturing just 5% of T-REX's initial $100B and shielding it via Zama would make Zama the largest privacy protocol by dollars encrypted in crypto history—larger than Zcash.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Quantifies how small a capture rate flips Zama to category leadership; a low-threshold bull case and a yardstick (dollars-encrypted) for the privacy sector.
insights.quote: if only 5% of that initial TL just 5% gets shielded with Zama it would make Zama a bigger privacy protocol by dollar encrypted than any privacy protocol in the history of crypto including Dcash

insights.claim: Hindi argues the core value of RWA tokenization is composability—mixing tokenized assets, using one as collateral for another, recreating the entire derivatives market on-chain—which requires a shared public ledger with atomic settlement, something impossible if each RWA sits on a separate private chain.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Composability requirement is the structural reason RWAs migrate to public chains over private ones—central to the public-chain infra investment case.
insights.quote: the whole point of RWAS is not just to trade the RWA is to actually compose them together to create derivative products

insights.claim: Hindi contends RWA tokenization opens a pie 'orders of magnitude larger' than native DeFi TVL, reframing crypto from fighting over a fixed DeFi pie to capturing trillions of traditional-finance assets moving on-chain over the next four years.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Reframes the addressable market; supports overweighting RWA-rails/infra over zero-sum DeFi-native competition.
insights.quote: trillions are moving on chain... like we had billions before

insights.claim: Zama identifies three high-demand privacy segments: (1) real-world assets (RWAs), (2) B2B payments, and (3) crypto-institutional finance (professional traders, liquid funds, VCs, exchanges).
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: A taxonomy for where on-chain privacy demand concentrates; useful map for sizing the privacy-infra TAM.

insights.claim: Hindi argues B2B payments—not retail payments—are the lucrative privacy use case: low-frequency but high-value (supplier payments in thousands, cross-border subsidiary balancing in millions), and companies do not want payroll or supplier payments leaked publicly.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Points investors toward B2B/enterprise stablecoin payment rails over high-volume retail payments as the value-capture layer.
insights.quote: B2B payments, on the other hand, less frequent transactions, huge value

insights.claim: Public on-chain wallets create signaling/front-running risk for large holders: a VC holding 1–3% of a token's supply gets front-run when moving to an exchange, forcing sales at lower prices—confidential tokens remove this signaling by hiding wallet identity and amounts.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Confidential-token adoption could materially change large-holder execution and on-chain transparency analytics; relevant to trading-flow and surveillance-based strategies.
insights.quote: The second the second you're moving those tokens to an exchange... the market has panicked, they frontr run you, the token price goes down

themes.theme: Moats: integrations, network effects, AI-era defensibility

insights.claim: Zama's core focus for 2026 is integrations—custody solutions, wallets, on/off-ramps, stablecoins, DeFi protocols, and exchanges—because shielded assets have no retention unless holders can swap, earn yield, trade, and move them.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Integration breadth (not the core tech) is the stated 2026 execution KPI and stickiness driver to track for Zama.
insights.quote: in order to have retention of shielded dollars, you need to provide as much utility and integrations as possible

insights.claim: Zama announced a partnership with Fireblocks (MPC custody-grade self-custody, used as an API by banks, market makers, and fintechs), letting all Fireblocks customers integrate confidential token flows—confidential payments, swaps, and earnings—as easily as standard ERC20 tokens.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Distribution via Fireblocks' API reach is a named go-to-market lever; an integration milestone for the adoption thesis.

insights.claim: Hindi frames Zama's durable moats as (1) network effects of shielded value (encrypted dollars don't port to another privacy protocol), (2) integrations that are hard to replicate, and (3) 28 filed patents—arguing the marginal cost of building software is trending to zero so code itself is no moat.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: In an AI-commoditized-software world, value-locked network effects + IP + integrations are the defensible moats—a lens for evaluating any infra protocol.
insights.quote: If someone is encrypting a dollar in Zama, it doesn't work with another privacy protocol... that creates a network effect of money

insights.claim: Hindi says he won't hire another engineer until his whole team has integrated AI coding agents (to find the gaps), but continues hiring aggressively in two areas: security (no compromise) and business development.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: A concrete signal of where human headcount value concentrates as AI commoditizes engineering—security and BD over generic dev roles.
insights.quote: we're not hiring a single engineer until everybody has integrated AI so that we know where the gaps are on the engineering side

insights.claim: Hindi argues that as AI drives the marginal cost of software toward zero, the winners in software and blockchain will be whoever has the most network effects and brand recognition, plus the human relationships behind enterprise deals that AI cannot replace.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Reorients competitive analysis of AI-era infra toward distribution, brand, and relationships rather than technical novelty.
insights.quote: whoever has the most network effect and the most brand recognition is going to win

insights.claim: Hindi argues FHE enables 'programmable compliance' superior to ZK: ZK forces a binary see-everything-or-nothing model, whereas with Zama a token issuer can define in the smart contract who can decrypt which encrypted data, tailoring compliance per asset and service.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: A specific technical differentiation (FHE vs ZK) that Hindi claims is unique to Zama; a competitive edge claim to weigh in the privacy-tech stack.
insights.quote: contrary to ZK that kind of forces you into this very binary you see everything or you see nothing kind of compliance world. FHE and Zama allows the service provider the token issuer to decide what rules and compliance they need

insights.claim: Hindi positions Zama as a pragmatic, compliance-friendly privacy protocol explicitly for legitimate financial use cases—differentiating from earlier cypherpunk permissionless privacy projects that enabled bad actors—and treats configurable compliance as part of the product.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Regulatory pragmatism is the wedge for institutional adoption and distinguishes Zama from sanctioned-risk privacy tools; relevant to regulatory-risk assessment.
insights.quote: the Zama protocol is for legitimate financial use cases and we've made it clear that we'll take every step that we can to prevent people from... abusing the protocol

insights.claim: Hindi asserts FHE (and therefore Zama) is already quantum-resistant because it uses the same lattice-type cryptography standardized by the US government for post-quantum communications—so data encrypted on a public chain via Zama today cannot be decrypted even by future quantum computers.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Post-quantum readiness is positioned as a structural edge over ZK (most ZK is not post-quantum); a differentiator for long-horizon security buyers.
insights.quote: fortunately FHE is already quantum resistant... the same type of cryptography that has been standardized by the American government for postquantum secure communications

insights.claim: Hindi predicts that any L1 not offering post-quantum signatures within 18 months is 'dead'—not because quantum will break it that fast, but because institutions will price in the failure to react to threats and move away.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: A concrete 18-month deadline by which to judge L1s on post-quantum roadmaps; a screening criterion for chain-level allocation.
insights.quote: my conviction is if an L1 does not offer postquantum signatures in the next 18 months, they're dead

insights.claim: Hindi argues the post-quantum migration is a governance problem, not a technical one: the technical solutions for post-quantum Bitcoin and Ethereum exist, but the hard question is what to do with un-migrated coins (burn, recycle as mining rewards, redistribute pro-rata) and whether Satoshi's coins move.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Reframes quantum risk as a governance/coordination overhang for Bitcoin and Ethereum—watch governance decisions, not just tech, as the catalyst.
insights.quote: The bottleneck is honestly it's a governance decision on what to do, what cut off date for losing your coins

insights.claim: Hindi cites the estimate that ~one-third of all bitcoins (~6 million, ~$600 billion at $100k each) are lost to forgotten keys, making the post-quantum cut-off decision an existential governance threat for Bitcoin specifically.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Sizes the lost-coin overhang that any Bitcoin post-quantum migration must resolve; a tail-risk variable for BTC holders.
insights.quote: the estimate is about a third of bitcoins are lost because people lost their keys

insights.claim: Hindi notes ERC20 tokens are often upgradable, so issuers can take action to migrate to post-quantum, contrasting with Bitcoin where lost coins cannot be recovered—making quantum less of an existential threat for upgradable token ecosystems than for Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Differentiates quantum risk across asset types; upgradable-token ecosystems have a migration escape hatch Bitcoin lacks.

insights.claim: Hindi says post-quantum readiness is now 'table stakes' in institutional conversations—if you don't check the box you're not even in the discussion—whereas many crypto-native OG founders still don't take quantum seriously.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Signals an institutional procurement filter forming around post-quantum; a gating requirement for vendors selling to TradFi.
insights.quote: like table stakes you know if you don't take the postquantum readiness box you're not even in the conversation

themes.theme: Latency vs. instant settlement as security

insights.claim: Hindi makes the contrarian case that crypto's instant settlement is a security liability and the industry will trade instant settlement for latency: most hacks could have been mitigated if large transactions took hours to settle, giving humans time to react and revert.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: If latency-as-security takes hold, it reshapes DeFi UX and risk design; a directional thesis against the instant-finality dogma.
insights.quote: we've been kind of redpilled into thinking that instant settlement was necessary for crypto to survive... most of the hacks that happened could have been prevented with some kind of latency

insights.claim: Hindi argues prevention of hacks is delusional (especially with AI) and the right security goal is mitigating impact—everybody gets hacked eventually, so the real question is how bad it is when it happens.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: A mitigation-over-prevention security philosophy that favors latency/reversibility designs; lens for evaluating protocol risk posture.
insights.quote: Preventing a hack is delusional especially with AI today.

insights.claim: Hindi proposes a market-based fix for latency: instead of instant settlement at the protocol level, let third parties run an instant-settlement market on top of delayed settlement (as bridges already do for optimistic rollups' 7–14 day withdrawal delays), charging a 1–5% fee to take the latency risk.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Identifies a fee-bearing instant-settlement-as-a-service market layered on delayed settlement—an emerging design pattern and potential business model.
insights.quote: let people actually create a market for instant settlement on top of delayed settlements

insights.claim: Hindi argues encrypting all wallets via Zama would improve security by removing attacker targeting information (nobody can see which wallets hold more), though it cannot protect a single signing wallet from social-engineering hacks like the six-month Drift compromise.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Privacy reduces attacker target-selection but not operational/social-engineering risk—useful nuance on what FHE does and doesn't secure.

insights.claim: Hindi frames the current disconnect between institutional adoption and negative crypto-social sentiment as the industry maturing: now that the US is 'open for business,' crypto companies are being measured on the same KPIs as any company—utility, revenue, adoption, growth—with multiples of ~10–20x recurring revenue.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Signals a fundamentals-driven repricing of crypto equities/tokens on revenue multiples rather than narrative; relevant to valuation frameworks.
insights.quote: it's uh 10 to 20x uh recurring revenue uh 100x if you're Elon Musk and you're promising data centers in space

insights.claim: Hindi divides crypto businesses into three categories: (1) successful token businesses (e.g. Hyperliquid), (2) crypto-rails businesses that IPO'd or sit adjacent to TradFi (e.g. Coinbase, Circle), and (3) token-based businesses struggling with product-market fit or a token that never made sense.
insights.speaker: host (Jack)
insights.so_what: A taxonomy for sorting crypto businesses by viability; useful screen for separating durable token models from broken ones.

insights.claim: Hindi predicts trillions of dollars will move on-chain within four years and that many beloved incumbent crypto protocols will become 'the Yahoos of crypto'—huge and loved today but irrelevant once the on-chain-finance era's equivalent of Google arrives.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: A warning that current protocol brands may not survive the TradFi transition—argues against assuming incumbency durability in crypto allocations.
insights.quote: a lot of the brands that we love, they're going to be the Yahoos of crypto

insights.claim: Hindi argues crypto-native risk management is structurally weaker than TradFi's: in TradFi a 0.01% chance of losing funds equals trillions and is unacceptable, while crypto treats million-dollar tail risks casually—so institutions weight low-probability events like quantum far more heavily.
insights.speaker: Rand Hindi (founder, Zama)
insights.so_what: Explains why institutional and crypto-native priorities diverge on tail risk—predicts institutional demand for security/post-quantum features the crypto base undervalues.
insights.quote: In TRFI, a 0.01% chance of losing your funds is trillions. Okay? In crypto, it's millions. The scale is just not the same.
